data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was caused by a systemic failure of the palatial redistribution economy due to seismic damage and supply chain fragmentation.", "mechanism": "A cluster of earthquakes (earthquake storm) destroyed key palace centers and ports, disrupting the tightly coupled bronze and grain trade networks. The rigid, centralized bureaucracies could not adapt to the sudden loss of surplus, leading to internal revolt and abandonment, whereas Egypt's more decentralized Nile-based agriculture allowed it to absorb the shock.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Mesopotamia"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows continuous palace administration in Pylos or Hattusa immediately following the proposed seismic event window.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This is the modal 'Earthquake Storm' theory, highly typical in current scholarship for explaining simultaneous destruction layers."}
